---
title: "1.3.4 Lossy and lossless file compression"
subtitle: "Data Representation P36-P44"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note {
          margin-top: 0.7em;
          padding: 0.42em 0.7em;
          border: 1px dashed #b8b8b8;
          color: #555;
          font-size: 0.72em;
        }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 1 Directory / 返回 Unit 1 目录](../../index.html)

## Lossy and Lossless File Compression

### Two types of file compression

File compression can either be <span class="term">lossless</span> (无损) or <span class="term">lossy</span> (有损).

## Lossy File Compression

### Some data is discarded

With this technique, the file compression algorithm <span class="mark">eliminates unnecessary data</span> from the file. This means the original file <span class="mark">cannot be reconstructed</span> once it has been compressed.

Lossy file compression results in <span class="mark">some loss of detail</span> when compared to the original file.

For example, when applying a lossy file compression algorithm to:

::: {.tight-list}
- an <span class="mark">image</span>, it may reduce the resolution and/or the bit/colour depth
- a <span class="mark">sound file</span>, it may reduce the sampling rate and/or the resolution
:::

Lossy files are <span class="mark">smaller than lossless files</span>, which is of great benefit when considering storage and data transfer rate requirements.

## Common Lossy Algorithms

### MP3, MP4 and JPEG

Common lossy file compression algorithms are:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">MPEG-3 (MP3)</span> and <span class="term">MPEG-4 (MP4)</span>
- <span class="term">JPEG</span>
:::

## MP3 and MP4

### Music and multimedia files

MP3 files are used for playing music on computers or mobile phones. This compression technology will reduce the size of a normal music file by about <span class="mark">$90\%$</span>. While MP3 music files can never match the sound quality found on a DVD or CD, the quality is satisfactory for most general purposes.

Essentially the algorithm removes sounds that the human ear cannot hear properly:

::: {.tight-list}
- removal of sounds <span class="mark">outside the human ear range</span>
- if two sounds are played at the same time, only the <span class="mark">louder one</span> can be heard by the ear, so the softer sound is eliminated
- this is called <span class="term">perceptual music shaping</span>
:::

MP4 files are slightly different to MP3 files. This format allows the storage of <span class="mark">multimedia files</span> rather than just sound - music, videos, photos and animation can all be stored in the MP4 format.

## JPEG

### Lossy compression for bitmap images

When a camera takes a photograph, it produces a <span class="mark">raw bitmap file</span> which can be very large in size. JPEG is a lossy file compression algorithm used for bitmap images.

The JPEG file reduction process is based on two key concepts:

::: {.tight-list}
- human eyes do not detect differences in <span class="mark">colour shades</span> quite as well as they detect differences in <span class="mark">image brightness</span>
- by separating pixel colour from brightness, images can be split into <span class="mark">$8 \times 8$ pixel blocks</span>
- certain information can then be <span class="mark">discarded</span> without causing any real noticeable deterioration in quality
:::

Once the image is subjected to the JPEG compression algorithm, a <span class="mark">new file</span> is formed and the original file can no longer be reconstructed.

## Lossless File Compression

### All original data can be reconstructed

With this technique, <span class="mark">all the data</span> from the original uncompressed file can be reconstructed.

This is particularly important for files where any loss of data would be disastrous, for example:

::: {.tight-list}
- transferring a <span class="mark">large and complex spreadsheet</span>
- downloading a <span class="mark">large computer application</span>
:::

Lossless file compression is designed so that <span class="mark">none of the original detail</span> from the file is lost.

## Run-Length Encoding (RLE)

### Lossless compression of repeated data

<span class="term">Run-length encoding (RLE)</span> can be used for lossless compression of a number of different file formats:

::: {.tight-list}
- it is a form of <span class="mark">lossless/reversible</span> file compression
- it reduces the size of a string of <span class="mark">adjacent, identical data</span>
- a repeating string is encoded into two values:
  - the first value represents the <span class="mark">number of identical data items</span> in the run
  - the second value represents the <span class="mark">code of the data item</span>
- RLE is only effective where there is a <span class="mark">long run of repeated units/bits</span>
:::

## Using RLE on Text Data

### `aaaaabbbbccddddd`

Consider the text string `aaaaabbbbccddddd`. Assuming each character requires `1 byte`, this string needs `16 bytes`.

If ASCII code is being used, the string can be coded as:

```text
05 97   04 98   02 99   05 100
```

This means:

::: {.tight-list}
- `5` characters with ASCII code `97`
- `4` characters with ASCII code `98`
- `2` characters with ASCII code `99`
- `5` characters with ASCII code `100`
:::

Assuming each number requires `1 byte`, the RLE code needs <span class="mark">`8 bytes`</span>. This is <span class="mark">half the original file size</span>.

## Using a Flag

### When a string has few repeats

One issue occurs with a string such as `cdcdcdcdcd` where RLE compression is not very effective. To cope with this, we use a <span class="term">flag</span>.

::: {.tight-list}
- a flag preceding data indicates that what follows are the <span class="mark">number of repeating units</span>
- for example, `255 05 97` means the flag is `255` and there are `5` items with ASCII code `97`
- when a flag is not used, the next byte(s) are taken with their <span class="mark">face value</span> and a run of `1`
:::

Introducing a flag (`255` in this case) produces:

```text
255 08 97   255 10 98   99 100 99 100 99 100   255 08 101
```

This has `15` values and would therefore require `15 bytes` of storage. This is a reduction in file size of about <span class="mark">$53\%$</span> when compared to the original string.

## Using RLE with Images

### Black and white image

Figure 1.12 shows the letter `F` in a grid where each square requires `1 byte` of storage. A white square has a value `1` and a black square a value of `0`.

::: {.figure-note}
**Figure 1.12** Using RLE with a black and white image placeholder
:::

In compressed RLE format this becomes:

```text
9W 6B 2W 1B 7W 1B 7W 5B 3W 1B 7W 1B 7W 1B 6W
```

Using `W = 1` and `B = 0` we get:

```text
91 60 21 10 71 10 71 50 31 10 71 10 71 10 61
```

The `8 × 8` grid would need `64 bytes`; the compressed RLE format has `30 values`, and therefore needs only <span class="mark">`30 bytes`</span> to store the image.

## Coloured Images

### Example 2

Figure 1.13 shows an object in four colours. Each colour is made up of <span class="term">red</span>, <span class="term">green</span> and <span class="term">blue</span> (`RGB`).

::: {.figure-note}
**Figure 1.13** Using RLE with a coloured image placeholder
:::

The original image (`8 × 8` square) would need <span class="mark">`3 bytes per square`</span> to include all three RGB values. Therefore, the uncompressed file for this image is:

<span class="mark">`8 × 8 × 3 = 192 bytes`</span>

The RLE code has `92 values`, which means the compressed file will be <span class="mark">`92 bytes`</span> in size. This gives a file reduction of about <span class="mark">$52\%$</span>.

## Classroom Check

### Understand compression types and RLE

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that <span class="term">lossy compression</span> removes some data, so the original file cannot be reconstructed
- that <span class="term">lossless compression</span> allows <span class="mark">all original data</span> to be reconstructed
- that MP3 and MP4 are used for <span class="mark">sound or multimedia</span>, and JPEG is used for <span class="mark">bitmap images</span>
- that <span class="term">RLE</span> stores a run as the <span class="mark">number of repeated items</span> together with the <span class="mark">code of the data item</span>
- that RLE is only effective where there is a <span class="mark">long run of repeated units/bits</span>
- that lossless compression is needed where <span class="mark">no data can be lost</span>, such as spreadsheets or applications
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Data Transmission

The next unit covers how data is transmitted.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 1 Directory / 返回 Unit 1 目录](../../index.html)
